stories.title: Systima Closes $153M Tax-Exempt CMBS Affordable Housing Deal
stories.excerpt: A private-label securitization closed by Systima Capital Management for a portfolio of nearly 1,300 subsided apartments underscores the potential for use of the commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) market in affordable housing deals. Systima closed the $153 million tax-exempt affordable housing bond deal through the Public Finance Authority securitized by a pool of loans on seven properties by the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) program. The transaction, which utilized tax-exempt CMBS debt for the 1,272-unit portfolio across Wisconsin, Illinois, Florida, Tennessee and Texas were “significantly oversubscribed” with more than $1.2 billion in orders from 19 institutional investors, according to Systima. Ryan Paszczykowski, head of structured investing at Systima, said the tax-exempt CMBS offering in the deal is poised to become more mainstream going forward thanks to strong investor appetite. J.P. Morgan led the transaction as the lead underwriter with Wells Fargo as co-manager, while Systima retained the subordinate Class B certificates. “I think institutional investors are starting to realize the credit protections that currently exist in affordable housing and the significant need for affordable housing,” Paszczykowski told CO. “I think the market is going to grow going forward.” Paszczykowski noted that tax-exempt CMBS financings have been used for affordable housing projects in the past but were typically “one off” that banks executed to free up their balance sheets. He said Systima is seeking to become one of the first dedicated managers to bring this capital markets strategy to the forefront as an avenue to deliver high-risk adjusted returns to investors. The original loan proceeds were used to fund acquisition, construction, rehabilitation and preservation of the affordable housing communities geared toward families earning at or below 60 percent of area median income. The exact locations and names of the individual properties
bullets: Systima closed $153M tax-exempt CMBS for 1,272-unit LIHTC portfolio across 5 states.
Deal oversubscribed 8x ($1.2B orders from 19 institutional investors) signals strong appetite.
Tax-exempt CMBS emerging as viable financing vehicle for affordable multifamily development.
stories.summary: Systima Capital Management closed a $153 million tax-exempt CMBS securitization backed by a 1,272-unit Low-Income Housing Tax Credit portfolio spanning Wisconsin, Illinois, Florida, Tennessee, and Texas. The deal attracted over $1.2 billion in bids from 19 institutional investors, representing an 8x oversubscription. J.P. Morgan led the underwriting, and Systima's leadership indicates this tax-exempt CMBS structure is poised to become mainstream for affordable housing transactions.
stories.impact: Heavy institutional demand for tax-exempt CMBS on LIHTC portfolios signals improved capital availability and lower cost of capital for affordable multifamily development. Developers pursuing LIHTC projects across secondary and tertiary markets should lock in construction and permanent financing windows now, as this precedent will likely accelerate institutional capital flows into subsidy-layered deals and reduce pricing pressure on future transactions.

stories.title: M&T RCC Provides $141M Bridge Loan to Newly Built Turtle Bay Apartments
stories.excerpt: M&T Realty Capital Corporation (M&T RCC) closed on a $141.4-million bridge loan for Anagram Turtle Bay, a newly constructed 194-unit multifamily community located at 300 E. 50th Stt. in Manhattan’s Turtle Bay neighborhood. The financing was provided to an ownership group led by Global Holdings, MAG Partners, and Safanad. The three-year bridge loan, which includes an extension option, refinanced the property’s construction financing and is designed to support the asset through the final stages of lease-up and stabilization before a planned transition to long-term permanent financing. “Bridge financing plays an increasingly important role in today’s market, particularly for newly delivered, high-quality multifamily assets that are approaching stabilization,” said Joe Pizzutelli, M&T RCC head of national production, “Anagram Turtle Bay represents exactly the type of opportunity where bridge capital can create value by providing flexibility and certainty of execution while the property completes its lease-up and transitions to permanent financing.” The post M&T RCC Provides $141M Bridge Loan to Newly Built Turtle Bay Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: M&T RCC closed $141.4M bridge loan for newly-built 194-unit Turtle Bay Manhattan property.
Three-year bridge with extension option funds lease-up and stabilization before permanent takeout.
Bridge financing gaining traction for stabilizing new construction multifamily seeking refinance windows.
stories.summary: M&T Realty Capital closed a $141.4 million bridge loan for Anagram Turtle Bay, a 194-unit newly constructed apartment community at 300 E. 50th Street in Manhattan's Turtle Bay neighborhood. The three-year bridge, extended-term option structure funds the property through lease-up and stabilization ahead of long-term permanent financing. The deal underscores growing reliance on bridge capital to navigate intermediate risk between construction and permanent debt markets.
stories.impact: Bridge financing for newly-delivered, near-stabilization assets reflects a tightening permanent debt market and elongated lease-up timelines in Manhattan due to elevated supply and competitive lease rates. Developers should expect bridge lenders to demand faster absorption and lower initial basis to justify pricing; properties requiring 24+ months to stabilize may face higher bridge costs or refinance risk in Manhattan, making pre-leasing and demand signals critical in the acquisition underwriting phase.

stories.title: Todd Rosenberg’s Pebb Capital Buys South Beach Office Building
stories.excerpt: Todd Rosenberg’s Pebb Capital has purchased the Uno office building in South Beach, as boutique financial firms vie for outposts in Miami. The seven-story building features about 100,000 square feet of leasable space and 106 covered parking spots at 119 Washington Avenue, between First and Second streets, in the upscale South of Fifth neighborhood. While the sale price remains unclear, it’s likely north of $69 million since Acore Capital provided a loan for that amount, property records show. A representative of Pebb Capital has not responded to a request for comment. Property records show an entity named Yantra 119, managed by Longstead at The Corcoran Group’s Cyril Bijaoui, has owned the building since 2011. Bijaoui, who manages and leases the property, declined to comment. During the pandemic, the seller renovated the interiors of the building, which was constructed in 2002. Earlier this month, the entity launched a construction project to add about 10,000 square feet of office space by converting a terrace. The sale comes as South Beach is seeing an uptick in new Class A office buildings, with demand coming from wealthy executives who want to work near their homes. The Fifth, a nearby boutique building backed by Google billionaire Eric Schmidt, is nearing completion after securing a $47.3 million construction loan in 2024. Hedge fund J. Goldman & Company signed a 10,000-square-foot lease, and Sant Ambroeus will open a 7,000-square-foot restaurant on the ground floor. Across the street, the same development team, Sumaida + Khurana and Bizzi + Bilgili, have another luxury office project, dubbed 1100 Fifth Street, in the pipeline. Further north in Sunset Harbour, private equity giant Ares Management opened a 10,500-square-foot office at the five-story Eighteen Sunset development, which was completed in 2024. Boca Raton-based Pebb Capital is making inroads in Miami. In January, the firm purchased a mixed-use building in the Miami Design District for $73 mi
bullets: Pebb Capital acquired office building (Uno) in South Beach Miami for $69M+ through Acore loan.
Boutique financial firms driving office demand in South Beach and South of Fifth submarket.
Conversion of office to other uses not reported; office market remains active in premium South Florida locations.
stories.summary: Todd Rosenberg's Pebb Capital acquired the Uno office building at 119 Washington Avenue in South Beach's South of Fifth neighborhood, with Acore Capital providing at least $69 million in financing. The seven-story, 100,000 sq ft building, previously renovated during the pandemic and undergoing terrace-to-office conversion work, reflects demand from boutique financial firms seeking Miami outposts.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance. However, sustained office demand from fintech and financial services firms in premium South Beach locations signals continued economic vitality and high-income job migration into Miami, which supports rental demand and rent growth in nearby multifamily submarkets, particularly Class-A properties in Brickell, Wynwood, and Downtown Miami.

stories.title: 1929-Vintage Los Feliz Apartments Sell to Dream Street Capital
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap has completed the sale of 1939 N. Kenmore Ave., an 18-unit multifamily property located in Los Angeles. The property sold for $5.28 million. “Properties with this charm and character that have been owned by the same family for several decades rarely come to market in Los Feliz,” said Jeff Louks, executive managing director investments. “The level of interest throughout the competitive sale process reflects continued demand for well-located multifamily assets. Investor activity remains steady, and we continue to close multiple transactions each month.” Louks and Elliot Sabag, investment specialists in Marcus & Millichap’s Encino office, represented the buyer, Michael Maltzman of Dream Street Capital. The seller was a private seller. Built in 1929, the property totals 18,317 square feet and is situated on a 0.26-acre parcel in Los Feliz. The Spanish-style building had been owned by the same family for several decades before the sale. The buyer plans to complete a renovation and restoration program. The post 1929-Vintage Los Feliz Apartments Sell to Dream Street Capital appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Dream Street Capital acquired 18-unit 1929-built property in Los Feliz for $5.28M.
Property sold for $293K per unit; family-held for decades now transitioning to active investor.
Los Feliz small-multi attracting investor capital despite age; renovation and value-add thesis intact.
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of 1939 N. Kenmore Ave., an 18-unit Spanish-style apartment building built in 1929 in Los Feliz, for $5.28 million ($293K/unit). The property, family-owned for decades, sold to Dream Street Capital, which plans a renovation and restoration program. The sale reflects continued investor appetite for vintage small-multifamily assets in desirable Los Angeles neighborhoods.
stories.impact: Los Feliz small-multi pricing and demand remain resilient, signaling that heritage property with character in prime LA submarkets continue to attract value-add investors. However, $293K per unit at 18 units suggests limited per-unit leverage; developers should focus acquisition activity on larger portfolios (30+ units) to achieve scale economics on capital stack and repositioning costs, particularly in aging stock where renovation timelines and labor costs are rising.

stories.title: New York People and Company News, Week of July 24, 2026
stories.excerpt: JLL announced that retail real estate veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy have joined the firm’s New York Retail team. Strauss joins as Executive Managing Director and Zinovoy joins as Senior Vice President, and they will work alongside Vice Chairman Patrick A. Smith to expand JLL’s retail leasing capabilities across New York and the country. Cohen & Steers, Inc. appointed Erik Schneberger as Executive Vice President, Chief Marketing Officer. Based in New York, Mr. Schneberger will lead the firm’s global marketing department, overseeing all marketing initiatives across distribution channels and regions worldwide. Schneberger joins Cohen & Steers with over 20 years of marketing experience, building and leading award-winning marketing organizations at some of the industry’s most recognized investment firms. A&G Real Estate Partners, a national real estate advisory firm specializing in lease optimization, lease renegotiations, real estate sales, and strategic real estate solutions, announced that Andrew Jacobs has joined the firm to lead its real estate sales division, effective immediately. Jacobs will report to Emilio Amendola, A&G’s Co-Founder and Co-CEO, and will be based out of the firm’s New York City office. PKF O’Connor Davies, one of the nation’s largest accounting, tax and advisory practices, announced that Anthony DelValle, CPA, has joined the organization as Partner in its Real Estate Tax practice. He is based in the Firm’s New York City office. DelValle brings more than 25 years of experience providing tax planning, compliance and consulting services with a focus on real estate and partnership taxation. DLC announced that Basil Donnelly has been named Chief Financial, Legal, and Business Officer, expanding his executive leadership responsibilities as the company continues its growth as one of the nation’s leading owner-operators of open-air shopping centers. A member of Team DLC since 20
bullets: JLL hired 2 senior retail veterans (Strauss, Zinovoy) to expand NYC retail leasing.
Cohen & Steers appointed chief marketing officer; multiple senior hires in NY real estate advisory.
Talent consolidation and hiring in NY-based real estate advisory and capital markets firms.
stories.summary: JLL announced the hire of Joshua Strauss (Executive Managing Director) and Scott Zinovoy (Senior Vice President) to bolster its New York retail leasing team. Concurrent hires at Cohen & Steers, A&G Real Estate Partners, and PKF O'Connor Davies reflect a broader talent consolidation among top-tier real estate advisory and capital markets firms in New York.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Generic personnel news in capital markets and advisory services does not signal meaningful shifts in demand, supply, capital availability, or development feasibility for multifamily assets.

stories.title: Merritt Picks Up 537K-SF Jax Industrial Park
stories.excerpt: Merritt Properties acquired Center Point Business Park, an 11-building, 537,800-square-foot light industrial park in Jacksonville’s Butler Corridor. The deal is the first major transaction announced since the company secured a $750 million investment led by Centerbridge Partners to accelerate its long-term growth strategy. Merritt Properties acquired Center Point from Plymouth REIT. Royce Rose and Justin Rabin of CBRE National Partners represented the seller in the transaction. The park is 99% leased to 35 tenants representing a diverse mix of industries. The property features 18- to 22-foot clear heights, rear-load configurations, dock-high loading in most suites, concrete tilt-wall construction, 107 dock doors and 1,005 parking spaces. The average tenant occupies approximately 15,100 square feet. Located at 4801-6631 Executive Park Court, the property is approximately 1.2 miles from Interstate 95 and one-half mile from the Florida East Coast Railway, providing last-mile connectivity throughout the market. The post Merritt Picks Up 537K-SF Jax Industrial Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Merritt Properties acquired 537.8K-SF light industrial park (11 buildings) in Jacksonville for undisclosed price.
Park 99% leased, 35 tenants, near I-95 and FEC Railway; first deal post-$750M Centerbridge funding.
Merritt growth-oriented via institutional capital; light industrial supply constrained and absorbed in JAX.
stories.summary: Merritt Properties acquired Center Point Business Park, an 11-building, 537,800 sq ft light industrial park in Jacksonville's Butler Corridor, from Plymouth REIT. The asset is 99% leased to 35 tenants with strong logistics connectivity (1.2 mi from I-95, 0.5 mi from FEC Railway). The deal marks Merritt's first major transaction following its $750 million Centerbridge Partners investment, signaling acceleration of growth-focused acquisitions.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. However, tight light industrial fundamentals and Centerbridge's $750M bet on Merritt signal strong institutional capital flow into real estate. This capital redeployment favors industrial over office and could tighten construction lending and labor availability in Jacksonville, raising soft costs for concurrent multifamily development in the market. Multifamily developers should accelerate JAX starts and lock labor before industrial expansion consumes local construction capacity.

stories.title: Greystar Receives State Funding Award to Redevelop Taunton’s Whittenton Mills
stories.excerpt: Greystar has received a $3.8-million Housing Development Incentive Program (HDIP) award from the Massachusetts Executive Office of Housing and Livable Communities to support the redevelopment of 437 Whittenton St. in Taunton, MA. The long-vacant Whittenton Mills site will be transformed into a 390-unit apartment community set to become the largest modular residential project in Massachusetts. HDIP is a state initiative that helps Gateway Cities such as Taunton develop market-rate housing while increasing residential growth and diversity of housing stock, supporting economic development, and promoting neighborhood stabilization. The program encourages new construction and the restoration of existing properties such as Whittenton Mills by providing funding to offset the cost of significant rehabilitation efforts. “Greystar is honored to receive this HDIP award that acknowledges the significance of the redevelopment at Whittenton Mills and the site’s potential to drastically expand housing opportunities in the City of Taunton,” said Ryan Souls, Managing Director of Development at Greystar. “This incentive from the Commonwealth is a testament to the role that Whittenton Mills will play in addressing the housing shortage and fully revitalizing a historic site that has been a pillar of this community for generations.” The post Greystar Receives State Funding Award to Redevelop Taunton’s Whittenton Mills appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Greystar awarded $3.8M HDIP state grant for 390-unit modular project at Whittenton Mills, Taunton, MA.
Largest modular residential project in MA; vacant mill redevelopment addressing housing shortage.
State subsidies and tax incentives reducing development risk and improving feasibility for Gateway City projects.
stories.summary: Greystar received a $3.8 million Housing Development Incentive Program (HDIP) award from Massachusetts to support the redevelopment of Whittenton Mills in Taunton into a 390-unit modular apartment community. Positioned as the largest modular residential project in the state, the award offsets rehabilitation costs at the long-vacant mill site and supports Taunton's Gateway City housing goals.
stories.impact: State housing incentive programs in secondary/tertiary markets (Taunton, MA) are expanding and incentivizing modular construction to reduce timelines and costs. Developers targeting Gateway Cities in Northeast should prioritize HDIP and similar programs; state subsidies improve IRR and risk profile, particularly for mill conversions and modular delivery. Modular construction gaining regulatory acceptance creates opportunity for streamlined development in markets like Taunton, Providence, and Springfield where traditional construction costs and labor constraints are acute.

stories.title: AI Firm Legora Takes 98K SF at SL Green’s 11 Madison Avenue
stories.excerpt: Legora, a company that developed a collaborative artificial intelligence-driven workspace for the legal profession, has signed a new 10-year lease for 98,420 square feet — the entire 11th floor — at SL Green Realty’s 11 Madison Avenue. This brings the building to 100 percent occupancy. SL Green was represented by Brian Waterman, Brent Ozarowski and Erik Harris at Newmark. Legora was represented by Justin Haber and Kyle Riker at JLL. The deal seems to represent a new office for Sweden-based Legora, which opened its New York City headquarters in 27,238 square feet at 838 Broadway last year. The asking rent was unclear, but the office asking rent at 11 Madison Avenue was $90 per square foot as recently as March. Legora’s lease brings the total square footage of office leases for SL Green, Manhattan’s largest office landlord, to roughly 1.5 million square feet so far in 2026. The company’s office properties in the borough maintain a pipeline of over 900,000 square feet. “We are excited to welcome another premier tenant to the already impressive tenant roster at 11 Madison Avenue, which includes Sony, UBS, Jim Beam Brands, WME and Pinterest,” said Steven Durels, executive vice president and director of leasing and real property at SL Green. “This new lease is testament to the building’s status as one of the most prominent properties in the exciting Midtown South neighborhood, and further evidence of the incremental demand that AI and technology tenants are bringing to an already strong leasing market.” 11 Madison Avenue serves as a home base for a number of high-level tech tenants, fitting perfectly into the Flatiron District’s tech-friendly area around Madison Square Park. In June 2025, Pinterest signed an 83,000-square-foot lease for 11 years for the building’s entire 13th floor for an asking rent of $90 per square foot. Health technology company Tempus AI signed a lease for 39,565 square feet for 10 years in August of that year, and March 2026 saw the AI-driven sales
bullets: Legora (AI legal workspace firm) signed 10-year lease for 98.4K SF (11th floor) at SL Green's 11 Madison Ave.
Tech tenant commits to full-floor lease at $90/SF+ asking rent; supports SL Green's Manhattan office occupancy.
Manhattan office leasing momentum accelerates with tech/legal tenants taking full floors in premium addresses.
stories.summary: Legora, a Swedish AI-driven legal workspace platform, signed a 10-year lease for 98,420 square feet (entire 11th floor) at SL Green's 11 Madison Avenue at an asking rent of approximately $90+ per square foot. The lease represents a major commitment from a growth-stage tech tenant and contributes to SL Green's 1.5 million sq ft of leasing activity and 900,000+ sq ft pipeline in Manhattan for 2026.
stories.impact: Sustained office leasing momentum in Manhattan Class-A towers signals stabilization of Manhattan's office market, reducing immediate conversion/redevelopment pressure on large trophy buildings. However, this benefits only prime trophy assets; secondary and tertiary office buildings continue to face structural headwinds. Multifamily developers should avoid depending on large-scale office-to-residential conversions in Manhattan for near-term supply relief; rather, focus on organic multifamily demand from population inflow and expect competition for capital with stabilizing office assets.

stories.title: SL Green Reports Improved Leasing Metrics in Q2 2026
stories.excerpt: SL Green is reaping the rewards of a resurgent New York City office market. “It might be the dead of summer, but our team is, of course, hard at work — this is truly when we shine the brightest, are completely dialed in on our business plan, and outwork the marketing,” said CEO Marc Holliday, Thursday afternoon during SL Green’s second-quarter earnings call. Holliday had earlier predicted the firm’s occupancy would rise — and SL Green saw its occupancy rate rise to 94.7 percent for its New York office portfolio, up from 94.4 percent . “Much of what we predicted at our investor conference in December has now played out in ways that improve cash flow,” he said, before listing off the latest shareholder metrics. The firm reported that its funds from operations (FFO) reached $109.6 million, or $1.43 per share, in the second quarter of 2026, compared to the $124.5 million, or $1.63 per share, recorded in the same period last year. The firm’s total revenue increased from $241 million in the second quarter of 2025 to $264 million in the second quarter of 2026, an increase of 9 percent, SL Green reported. The firm reported a net loss of $26.5 million, or 38 cents per share, in the second quarter of 2026, compared to the net loss of $11.1 million, or 16 cents per share, one year ago. The New York-based real estate investment trust (REIT) signed 53 Manhattan office leases in the second quarter of 2026 that totaled 445,161 square feet, with an average rent of $93.17 per square foot and average lease term of 5.8 years, bringing its leasing activity through the first six months of 2026 to 1,374,425 square feet leased in 104 Manhattan office transactions. “A growing scarcity of premier space in desirable Midtown districts has turned the tables in our favor,” said Holliday, who added the firm expects to exceed its internal leasing goals by the end of the year. “The trend continues to move in the right direction.” The flagship deals SL Green inked in the second quarter includ
bullets: SL Green reported Q2 2026 occupancy rise to 94.7% (from 94.4%); FFO $1.43/share vs $1.63 prior year.
Revenue increased to $264M (Q2 2026) from $241M (Q2 2025); leasing momentum accelerating mid-year.
Manhattan office fundamentals improving, but FFO per-share declining year-over-year signals unit growth/dilution.
stories.summary: SL Green reported Q2 2026 results showing occupancy improvement to 94.7% (from 94.4% in Q2 2025) and total revenue growth to $264 million (from $241 million prior year). However, FFO per share declined to $1.43 (from $1.63 prior year), indicating dilution despite occupancy and revenue gains. CEO Marc Holliday attributed gains to predictable business execution and market recovery aligned with December 2025 investor guidance.
stories.impact: Marginal impact on multifamily. SL Green's occupancy improvement and leasing momentum signal stabilization of Manhattan office, reducing urgency for large-scale office-to-residential conversions. However, FFO-per-share decline despite nominal revenue growth suggests share dilution or margin compression; capital deployment in Manhattan real estate remains challenging, keeping institutional capital competitive and reducing availability for multifamily expansion in the metro.

stories.title: John Farina of U.S. Development: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: As Pompano Beach, Fla., turns into a luxury condo market, U.S. Development has found success selling high-end units on the ocean. The company has completed construction of Salato, a 40-unit project close to the shore at 305 Briny Avenue, and sold more than 70 percent of the units, which are priced from $2.4 million to $5.3 million. Salato’s amenities and services include housekeeping with linen service, grocery pickup and stocking, spa and beauty treatments at the on-site Salato Spa, fitness classes, personal training and beach yoga, said John Farina, CEO of U.S. Development. The following conversation has been edited for length and clarity.   Commercial Observer: Pompano Beach wasn’t a luxury market for a long time. What prompted you to do a high-end project there? John Farina: When we started looking at the South Florida market in general, Pompano Beach was really the next shoe to drop. It’s nestled between Boca Raton and Delray Beach and Fort Lauderdale, and it was inevitable that this market was bound to really have growth. And we weren’t the first to come in there. You saw the Ritz-Carlton came in right before us, and we saw that the opportunity was there. Pompano has always been a sleepy little town, but when you really take a look at it, you see some of the nicest beaches in southeast Florida. We said, “Wow, there’s some great opportunity here. The prices aren’t as high.” There’s a lot more buyers in our pool in Pompano Beach, and we really thought it was a destination that was up and coming, and it was a great opportunity for us. What makes Salato unique? What’s drawing a different type of buyer is that we’re really focused on privacy and intimacy. With only 40 units, we have four semi-private elevators. We have 14-foot-deep terraces, which you don’t really see in larger buildings. We have 20,000 square feet of amenities for only 40 units, and when you’re there, you really feel like it’s almost
bullets: U.S. Development completed 40-unit luxury condo project (Salato) in Pompano Beach, 70%+ sold at $2.4M–$5.3M.
Pompano Beach transitioning from secondary to luxury market; located between Delray/Boca/Fort Lauderdale.
South Florida coastal amenity-driven projects absorbing high-net-worth demand; rental multifamily remains separate demand stream.
stories.summary: U.S. Development completed Salato, a 40-unit luxury condo project at 305 Briny Avenue in Pompano Beach, with over 70% of units sold priced $2.4M–$5.3M. CEO John Farina characterized Pompano Beach as an emerging luxury market, positioned between established luxury markets (Delray Beach, Boca Raton, Fort Lauderdale), driven by geographic inevitability and developer vision. Amenities include on-site spa, housekeeping, and fitness services.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance; condo product and luxury ownership market operate separately from rental multifamily. However, Pompano Beach's emergence as luxury destination signals broader South Florida wealth migration and coastal demographic shifts that will support rental demand in adjacent lower-price-point multifamily. Developers should monitor coastal supply/demand dynamics: condo absorption in Pompano may reduce investor capital available for rental multifamily, but also signals rising coastal rents as ownership product captures high-end demand.

stories.title: Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key, Live Local high-rise among $420M-plus South Florida construction permits for week ending July 22, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest permit issued was a $240 million new construction permit for 1 Island Drive in Miami, where Swire Properties is developing The Residences at Mandarin Oriental, Miami and Mandarin Oriental Hotel on Brickell Key. The former Mandarin Oriental Hotel towers on the site were demolished via controlled implosion in April. The redevelopment will include condos and a hotel, totaling about 1.35 million square feet. Job Filing Number: BD23028760001B001 📊 New Building: The second-largest permit was $95 million for 114 Northeast 22nd Street in Miami, where New Sense LLC, an affiliate of Argentina-based HA Emprendimientos, is developing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: $240M permit for Mandarin Oriental mixed-use (hotel + condo) on Brickell Key in Miami; ~1.35M SF total.
$95M permit for Sense 22 (320+ units, 36 stories) mixed-use in Miami with $111M S3 Capital construction loan.
South Florida $420M+ weekly permit pace; robust mixed-use and residential pipeline in Miami core markets.
stories.summary: South Florida issued $420M+ in construction permits for week ending July 22, 2026. Major permits include: (1) $240M for Swire Properties' Mandarin Oriental Residences Miami and hotel redevelopment on Brickell Key (1.35M SF mixed-use); (2) $95M for Sense 22, a 36-story, 320+ unit Live Local mixed-use project funded by $111M S3 Capital loan; (3) $21.3M for 112-unit mixed-use project in Miami's Health District; (4) $19M for City of Miami Administration Building as part of Miami Freedom Park redevelopment.
stories.impact: South Florida multifamily pipeline remains robust with strong lender support ($111M construction financing for 320-unit project signals active leverage availability). However, concentrated permitting velocity in Miami core (Brickell, Health District, Downtown) suggests land scarcity and intensifying competition for prime sites. Developers should prioritize off-peak locations (Naranja, Wynwood, Allapattah) and lock land positions quickly, as permitting pace indicates capital is flowing aggressively into South Florida and scarcity rents are rising. Monitor S3 Capital and similar lenders for construction loan appetite and terms.

stories.title: UrbanStreet targets former Motorola office for residential conversion
stories.excerpt: UrbanStreet Group is planning a residential conversion of an atrium office building that used to house Motorola, as part of Schaumburg’s the District at Veridian development. During an informal presentation with village officials, UrbanStreet managing partner Bob Burk said that converting the six-story, 360,200-square-foot building would be a “poster child” for redevelopment creativity, according to the Chicago Daily Herald. The project also includes the construction of a sister building that would reach as high as four stories, giving space for the complex to have around 300 units. 18 row houses will connect the complex to D.R. Horton’s Northgate at Veridian […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: UrbanStreet planning 300-unit residential conversion of former Motorola office building in Schaumburg.
District at Veridian mixed-use mega-development ($1B) on 225-acre former Motorola campus; first phase $175M cost.
Large-scale office-to-residential conversion enabled by aggregated land; structured master plan reducing per-unit entitlement risk.
stories.summary: UrbanStreet Group is converting a 360,200 sq ft, six-story former Motorola office building in Schaumburg into a ~300-unit apartment complex as part of the District at Veridian, a $1 billion mixed-use development on the 225-acre former Motorola campus. The conversion will anchor a larger campus plan including a sister four-story building, 30K SF retail, parking, 150-key hotel, and 18 row houses. UrbanStreet locked $94M Bank OZK construction financing in 2025 for Phase 1 ($175M cost); first apartment phase nearing completion (early 2027 timeline).
stories.impact: Suburban Chicago office-to-residential conversion demonstrates feasibility on aggregated mega-sites with master planning. Single-building conversions remain risky; UrbanStreet's approach (bundled retail, hotel, row houses, anchored by 300-unit residential) distributes risk and improves absorption. However, Schaumburg's distance from Chicago CBD limits appeal to young professionals; conversion will serve suburban renters and empty-nesters. Developers should replicate the bundled master-plan model rather than pursue siloed office conversions in secondary suburbs—mixed-use activation improves NOI and reduces concentration risk.

stories.title: As Deerfield loosens waterfront development rules, West Palm tightens them
stories.excerpt: Two South Florida coastal cities are moving in opposite directions on waterfront development, as localities grapple with regulating construction on the region’s most valuable real estate.  Deerfield Beach is considering looser guidelines for building on the waterfront, while West Palm Beach has temporarily reined in development.  Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 Tuesday for charter amendments that would loosen decadeslong restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and the Atlantic Ocean, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The change also would lift limits on mixed-use developments and projects with accessory use that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 to loosen waterfront FAR caps, mixed-use restrictions for multifamily/commercial.
West Palm Beach simultaneously tightening waterfront rules; zoning divergence creating misaligned development incentives.
Waterfront development entitlement easing in Deerfield; multifamily developers should prioritize permitting before Nov referendum.
stories.summary: Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 (preliminary) to loosen decades-old waterfront restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio (FAR) maximums for multifamily and commercial properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and Atlantic Ocean. Changes lift mixed-use development caps and require voter approval only for city property sales/leases >$750K. Final city vote scheduled for Aug. 4; residents will decide in Nov. 3 referendum. Concurrently, West Palm Beach is tightening waterfront development rules, signaling divergent coastal zoning strategies across South Florida.
stories.impact: Deerfield Beach waterfront FAR deregulation could unlock significant multifamily density if referendum passes in November. Developers should expedite site control and pre-permitting applications in Deerfield waterfront now to queue projects ahead of voter decision; post-referendum approval timelines may elongate if residents vote against zoning. Conversely, West Palm Beach tightening signals rising NIMBYism and regulatory risk in adjacent coastal markets—avoid speculative West Palm waterfront acquisitions pending regulatory clarity. Geographic zoning divergence creates arbitrage opportunity: rank Deerfield waterfront sites ahead of comparable West Palm assets.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning & HTG financed 115-unit affordable project (Artisan Pointe, Naranja) with $55.4M (LIHTC + CRA + mortgage).
$31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M Berkadia mortgage, $7M CRA funding (half tax-increment); 30/60/70% AMI targeting.
South Miami-Dade affordable multifamily absorbing subsidy layers; community development agency funding enabling below-market rents.
stories.summary: Alonzo Mourning (through AM Affordable Housing) and Housing Trust Group secured $55.4 million in financing for Artisan Pointe, a 115-unit affordable complex in unincorporated Naranja, Miami-Dade County, under construction with January 2028 completion. Financing comprised $31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M Berkadia permanent loan, and $7M Naranja Lakes CRA funding (half tax-increment). Units targeted to households at 30%, 60%, and 70% AMI, with one-bedroom rents ranging $766–$1,359 and two-bedroom reaching $2,145 versus local $1,350–$2,250 average.
stories.impact: South Miami-Dade LIHTC and CRA funding availability remains robust, signaling continued institutional appetite for subsidy-layered deals in secondary submarkets. However, Naranja's lower rents ($1,350–$2,250) versus Miami-Dade AMI ($89,800) indicate demand for workforce/affordable product. Developers pursuing LIHTC should focus Naranja and similar gateway communities where tax-increment funding and community development agencies are supportive and have reserves; avoid LIHTC competition in saturated markets (Wynwood, Little Haiti) where per-unit costs are rising due to land and entitlement competition.

stories.title: Endeavor boosts Austin office pipeline with 5th and Walsh project
stories.excerpt: Endeavor’s 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, which will also house its new headquarters, is finally getting off the ground.  Plans for the new five-story, 198,000-square-foot office mid-rise and retail building were submitted to the city in March 2022, more than four years ago. The development will feature 21,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space on the ground floor and a below-grade parking garage, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The project is expected to be completed in the later months of 2028, and Endeavor will promptly move into the building from its old headquarters at 500 West […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor developing 5th and Walsh office (198K SF, 5-story) in Downtown Austin; completion late 2028.
50%+ of building preleased (85K SF by Endeavor + unnamed tenants); ground-floor retail, below-grade parking.
Amazon in advanced talks to build robotics facility on Endeavor-owned Dog's Head property; 2,614-acre annexation approved.
stories.summary: Endeavor is developing 5th and Walsh, a 198,000 sq ft, five-story office mid-rise in Downtown Austin with completion targeted for late 2028. The project includes 21,000 SF retail/restaurant space and below-grade parking. Endeavor will occupy ~25,000 SF; additional 85,000 SF preleased to unnamed tenants, totaling 50%+ pre-leased before construction begins. Concurrently, Amazon is in advanced talks to occupy Endeavor-owned Dog's Head area (2,614 acres, recently annexed into city limits) as anchor for a robotics manufacturing facility and 45-year development agreement.
stories.impact: Amazon's potential robotics facility in Austin signals major job inflow and population migration into the metro. Ancillary multifamily demand will be robust in 2027–2030 timeframe as facility buildout attracts engineering, logistics, and service workers. Developers should lock land in corridors serving Dog's Head (northwest Austin) and Downtown Core before Amazon official announcement; land values will spike. However, note Downtown Austin office supply pipeline remains constrained (5th and Walsh is major downtown addition), so multifamily demand will concentrate in urban-core proximity and suburban office parks. Prioritize multifamily projects within 3–5 miles of Dog's Head and Downtown for maximum capture of Amazon-induced demand.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital filed data center permit (226K+ SF, Plaza 500 South) in Alexandria, VA on 34-acre site purchased 4 years ago for $165M.
Proposed substation within 100 feet of Bren Mar neighborhood homes; resident opposition and September planning commission review.
Data center development in Northern VA competing for land, utility capacity, and community acceptance alongside residential growth.
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group (Barry Sternlicht) filed a permit application for a two-building data center project at Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line (Virginia). The first phase, Plaza 500 South, is planned for 226,000+ sq ft with HKS as architect; Phase 1 construction cost estimated at $5M. The site, purchased four years ago for $165M, includes a proposed substation within 100 feet of homes in Bren Mar, sparking resident opposition and a planned September planning commission hearing. Starwood separately owns a data center campus under development in nearby Herndon.
stories.impact: Northern Virginia data center pipeline is accelerating (Starwood dual-site strategy: Herndon + Alexandria), signaling strong institutional demand for cloud/AI infrastructure. However, regulatory friction (resident opposition, substation siting disputes) and utility infrastructure constraints may delay permitting and increase soft costs. Multifamily developers in Northern VA should monitor data center buildouts: they consume significant electrical capacity, water, and land—potentially tightening utility availability and raising development costs for concurrent residential projects in Alexandria and Fairfax County. Lock utility commitments and expedite entitlements before data center demand saturates county infrastructure.
